did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid deep learning model combining a DenseNet201 backbone for global semantic feature extraction and a customized CNN module for local lesion texture analysis to classify malignant skin cancers (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Melanoma) using dermoscopic images from the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original experimental study proposing a novel hybrid deep learning framework (DenseNet201 + CNN) for skin cancer classification. It details specific methodology including dataset preprocessing (ISIC subset), data augmentation, model architecture, and training parameters (AdamW, 50 epochs). It reports original quantitative results (92% accuracy, 91.1% sensitivity, 95.5% specificity) and compares them against baseline models, which characterizes it as a primary research article rather than a review.

title: An Optimized Framework for Accurate Skin Cancer Classification
journal: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computation, Electronics, Power and Telecommunication (iConSCEPT)
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid deep learning framework for classifying three malignant skin cancers: Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Melanoma. The model combines a DenseNet201 backbone for global semantic feature extraction with a customized CNN module for local lesion texture analysis. The authors utilized a subset of the ISIC Skin Cancer Dataset, preprocessing 1,800 balanced images through resizing, normalization, and extensive data augmentation. Evaluation on a stratified test set demonstrated that the proposed model achieved an overall accuracy of 92%, with a sensitivity of 91.1% and specificity of 95.5%. Comparative analysis indicated that this hybrid approach significantly outperformed standalone DenseNet201, basic CNN, YOLOv7, and CNN + GoogleNet models in terms of accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three malignant skin cancer classes studied. The dataset initially contained 392 samples, which were balanced to 600 samples per class via augmentation. The model achieved a precision of 0.97 and recall of 0.84 for this class.

One of the three malignant skin cancer classes studied. The dataset initially contained 197 samples, which were balanced to 600 samples per class via augmentation. The model achieved a precision of 0.85 and recall of 0.93 for this class.

One of the three malignant skin cancer classes studied. The dataset initially contained 454 samples, which were balanced to 600 samples per class via augmentation. The model achieved a precision of 0.93 and recall of 0.97 for this class.

all_task_types.target: Malignant skin cancer classes (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of malignant skin lesions using dermoscopic images. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (BCC, SCC, MEL) using a softmax layer to output class probabilities. The task is explicitly defined as 'malignant skin lesion categorization' and 'classification of malignant skin cancers'.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Skin Cancer Dataset (Subset)
datasets_used.description: A subset of the ISIC Skin Cancer Dataset (specifically referenced as the 'ISIC 9-Class Skin Cancer Dataset' from Kaggle) containing dermoscopic images of malignant skin lesions. The study selected a specific portion limited to three malignant classes.

use.training: Used for training the hybrid DenseNet201 + CNN model after augmentation and class balancing.
use.validation: Used for validation (288 images, 16% of total).
use.testing: Used for testing/evaluation (360 images, 20% of total).
use.other: The original raw dataset contained 1043 images (392 BCC, 197 SCC, 454 MEL) before preprocessing and balancing.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To split the dataset into training, validation, and testing subsets while maintaining class balance.
To calculate and evaluate classification metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, and F1-score.
To compare the performance of the proposed hybrid model against baseline models (DenseNet201, basic CNN, YOLOv7, CNN + GoogleNet, ResNet150) using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, F1-score) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Skin Cancer Dataset (Subset).notes: The proposed model achieved superior detection (sensitivity) and classification reliability (specificity) compared to baselines. The text notes that YOLOv7 and Custom CNN had lower sensitivity, while CNN + GoogleNet had a substantial drop in sensitivity limiting clinical reliability. ResNet50 did not report sensitivity or specificity.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid Deep Learning Model (DenseNet201 + Custom CNN)
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained DenseNet201 model used as backbone for global semantic feature extraction

architecture_details.backbone: DenseNet201 with dense connection system for feature re-use
architecture_details.backbone_processing: Global Average Pooling (GAP) layer to obtain semantic vector
architecture_details.custom_module: Lightweight spatial CNN head (Spatial CNN Head)
architecture_details.feature_fusion: Concatenation of semantic vector (F_Dense) and spatial feature vector (F_CNN) via F_fusion = [F_Dense || F_CNN]
architecture_details.fusion_processing: Fully connected layers with dropout regularization

regularization: Dropout regularization in fully connected layers

preprocessing_techniques: Z-score normalization (using mean and standard deviation of the training set)
Data augmentation including geometric rotation (max 30°), height/width translation (max 15%), shear transformations, zooming (up to 20%), brightness changes (0.7-1.3), and two-axis flipping
Class balancing via oversampling of minority classes (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Basal Cell Carcinoma) to achieve 600 samples per class
Stratified sampling for splitting the dataset into training (64%), validation (16%), and testing (20%) subsets

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Height and width translations with a maximum of 15%.
Applied as part of the geometric augmentation pipeline.
Brightness changes with a range of 0.7 to 1.3.
Oversampling of minority classes (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Basal Cell Carcinoma) to achieve a balanced dataset of 600 samples per class, resulting in a total of 1,800 images.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid deep learning model, combining a DenseNet201 backbone for global semantic feature extraction and a customized CNN module for local lesion texture analysis, effectively addresses the limitations of conventional CNNs in capturing both high-level semantics and complex local textures simultaneously.

The model achieved an overall accuracy of 92%, with a sensitivity of 91.1% and specificity of 95.5% on a balanced subset of the ISIC Skin Cancer Dataset, demonstrating high diagnostic stability for Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Melanoma.

The hybrid approach significantly outperformed standalone DenseNet201, basic CNN, YOLOv7, CNN + GoogleNet, and ResNet150 models in terms of accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity, particularly overcoming the lower sensitivity issues found in YOLOv7 and CNN + GoogleNet.

The integration of comprehensive data augmentation and class balancing techniques (oversampling minority classes to 600 samples each) improved the model's generalization capabilities and prevented class bias, leading to reliable performance across all three malignant classes.

The high specificity (95.5%) of the model is clinically significant as it reduces the number of false positives, thereby decreasing the likelihood of unnecessary biopsies, while the high sensitivity (>90%) ensures that very few malignant cases are missed.

The study concludes that the proposed hybrid framework is a promising automated diagnostic tool for the early detection of malignant skin cancers, offering potential for real-time clinical application to assist dermatologists in diagnostic timeliness and treatment.
important_remarks: The study uses a highly restricted subset of the ISIC dataset (only 3 malignant classes: BCC, SCC, Melanoma) derived from the 'ISIC 9-Class Skin Cancer Dataset' on Kaggle, limiting the generalizability of findings to benign lesions or other malignancies.

Class imbalance was addressed via aggressive oversampling of minority classes (SCC and BCC) to achieve exactly 600 samples per class, resulting in a total of 1,800 images; this synthetic balancing may not reflect real-world clinical prevalence distributions.
Patient-level data was not available, so stratified sampling was performed at the image level, which risks data leakage if multiple images from the same patient appear in both training and test sets.
The paper does not report any formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) to validate the significance of performance improvements over baseline models.

External validation was not performed; the model was evaluated solely on a stratified split of the selected ISIC subset, raising concerns about generalizability to other dermoscopic image sources or populations.

Comparative baselines (YOLOv7, CNN+GoogleNet, ResNet50) were cited from other studies rather than re-trained and evaluated on the exact same preprocessed dataset, potentially introducing unfair comparison biases due to differing preprocessing or augmentation pipelines.

The dataset size (1,800 images) is relatively small for deep learning, relying heavily on data augmentation and transfer learning (DenseNet201) to achieve results, which may limit robustness in diverse clinical settings.
No ablation study is reported to isolate the specific contribution of the custom CNN module versus the DenseNet201 backbone to the overall performance gain.
